Admission Date:  1994-12-12              Discharge Date:   1991-01-03

Date of Birth:  1970-11-29             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Sara
Chief Complaint:
shortness of breath

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None


History of Present Illness:
83 F ho stage 3 CKD (Cr 1.5 baseline), HTN, CAD, who is being
transfered from Cardiovascular surgery to CCU for medical
management of severe aortic stenosis and acute renal failure.
Pt presented to Sci-waymart Forensic Treatment Center with shortness of breath. She
was told by her primary physician [**Name Initial (PRE) **] few days prior to
presentation to stop her lasix.
While at OSH, she was felt to have volume overload and was given
lasix 20mg IV initialy then switched to 40mg PO BID. Echo at OSH
showed mild concentric LVH, EF 50%, severe AS with mean gradient
31mmHg, 3306 Joseph Square Suite 327
Richardsonville, VT 15949-1778 0.75 sqcm. She then had cardiac cath on 09-20 (given
contrast load, unknown amount) which confirmed severe AS with
valve area 0.75 (unknown if they also evaluated coronaries).
Pt was then transfered to Shriners Hospital For Children yesterday for planned AVR. She
was noted to have Cr 2.2->3.3, bicarb 19, Na 126, K 5.5, and
pedal edema up to thighs. She was given 80 IV lasix overnight
(takes 40 po BID at home) and only put out only 20cc. Pt being
transfered to medical CCU for acute on chronic renal failure,
acute diastolic heart failure and AS.
Pt also noted to have positive UA and started on cipro.
When pt is medically optimized, plan is to take her to the OR
for AVR.

Notable OSH labs:
BNP increased to 5,000->13,000. Troponin 0.08->0.14->0.12. HCT
40, MCV 90, Ferritin 151, Fe 43, TIBC 229. Pt was given procrit
on 2000-01-26, per nephrology. Cr 1.5-> 2.2 prior to transfer.

On arrival to the CCU, pt is comfortable, no SOB, no chest pain.

Past Medical History:
1. CARDIAC RISK FACTORS: +Diabetes, +Dyslipidemia, +Hypertension

2. CARDIAC HISTORY:  none known
3. OTHER PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
heart murmur since childhood"
Hypothyroid
Gout
Anemia
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary disease- pt DENIES this
Osteoporosis

sudden cardiac death; otherwise non-contributory.

Brief Hospital Course:
83 F with history of CKD (stage 3, baseline Cr 1.5), severe
aortic stenosis, HTN, HLD, DM2, admitted to Shriners Hospital For Children for planned
AVR, now being transfered to CCU for management of severe AS,
acute diastolic heart failure and acute renal failure.

ACTIVE ISSUES:
#Acute on chronic Renal Failure: Pt has known CKD 3 with Cr
baseline 1.9 and Cr 3.3 on admission to the CCU concerning for
ARF. The differential included pre-renal in the setting of
volume overload and poor forward flow and intrinsic etiologies
including contrast nephropathy (recent contrast load sp cardiac
cath), cholesterol emboli (pt with severe AS and recent
manipulation of vasculature during cardiac cath), AIN (less
likely since no new medications), ATN (possible given pre-renal
state which can proceed to ATN). This was thought to be
multi-factorial with pre-renal, contrast nephropathy, and
possibly cholesterol emboli. ACE inhibitors and colchicine were
stopped. The patient was found to have elevated eosinophil
counts in her urine and in the context of no new drugs likely
causing AIN, cholesterol embolization was considered.
Fundoscopy, however, revealed no Hollenhorst plaques. The renal
service was consulted and felt that her kidney injury was likely
severe contrast nephropathy resulting in oliguric acute tubular
necrosis in the setting of severe AS. She remained oliguric and
was started on a furosemide drip to maintain her urine output.
By 1980-04-06, her Cr had increased to 5.0. We started metolazone
to help with diuresis and she eventually started putting out
urine. Eventually we stopped the lasix drip and the metolazone
because she was putting out good urine on her own. She had
elevated eosinophilia (20%) which can be seen in cholesterol
emboli. Her Cr plateaued at around 5 while here which his not
uncommon to see during Smith, especially ATN. Again the most
likely etiology for Smith was multifactorial: cholesterol emboli,
contrast nephropathy and ATN from CHF.

# Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: The patient
presented with pedal edema and pulmonary edema, suggestive of
acute diastolic heart failure. CXR on 2006-07-11 showed small
bilateral pleural effusions and pulmonary vascular congestion,
both of which had improved (after diuresis) on a repeat film
dated 1980-04-06. Her lung sounds improved while she was here.

# Severe Aortic Stenosis: Cath at OSH showed 3306 Joseph Square Suite 327
Richardsonville, VT 15949-1778 0.78 consistent
with severe AS. Pt initialy admitted to OSH for CHF in setting
of holding her home lasix, suggestive of symptomatic aortic
stenosis. An echocardiogram was obtained that showed severe
aortic stenosis and an ejection fraction of 55%. Cardiac surgery
deferred any intervention given her acute-on-chronic kidney
injury, as described above. She was medically managed in the
cardiac care unit with afterload reduction, as described above.
Surgery decided they did not want to operate till her kidney
function had returned to baseline.

# Hyponatremia: The patient was admitted with hyponatremia,
which worsened over her CCU course. She was thought to have
hypervolemic hyponatremia with poor forward flow, appropriately
increasing ADH (resulting in increased water reabsorption in
distal tubule and subsequent low Na). The patient was fluid
restricted to 500cc/day and diuresed. Looking at outside
hospital notes she had been hyponatremic even in early 15 with
Na down to 119. It is likely she has chronic hyponatremia. It
was also thought that adding the metolazone to help with
diuresis may have worsened her hyponatremia even more so that
was held. During hospital course she was for the most p art
asymptomatic though when her Na was down to 120 the lowerst it
was she was complaining of nausea and decreased appetite. Her Na
eventually went up to 126 an dshe felt less nauseous and had
more of an appetite.  She was discharged with a Na of 124 which
was felt to be stable.

# Positive UA: The patient's UA showed 33 epithelial cells, so
unlikely clean specimen. She was started on ciprofloxacin and
continued this course for four days and then it was
discontinued.

# HTN: The patient initially received Labetolol 100 Healthsouth Rehabilitation Hospital Of Southern Arizona and
amlodipine 2.5mg. Her lisinopril 40mg was held in setting of
Smith. In an effort to further decrease her afterload and improve
her forward flow, her labetalol was increased to 300 TID and
amlodipine to 10mg daily. We also started her on hydralazine
10mg q8h.

CHRONIC ISSUES:
# CAD: Unknown if there was any imaging of coronaries. Per OSH
records, it seems that only hemodynamics and aortic valve
measurements were taken. The patient's cardiac regimen was
continued with changes to her anti-hypertensive regimen as noted
above.

# DM2: A1C 6.0. Takes glargine 13 at home and was continued on
glargine 13 at breakfast and an insulin sliding scale.

#Gout: No acute episodes at this time. will hold home colchicine
and allopurinol in setting of Smith. Her colchicine and
allopurinol were held.

# HLD: the patient was continued on her atorvastatin 10mg

# Hypothyroidism: stable on synthroid 50 mcg. Of note, the
admitting surgical team had entered the patient's dose as 12.5
mcg, but this was rectified when the patient was in the cardiac
ICU.

#Anemia: pt has history of anemia, likely secondary to CKD as
she was given procrit on 07-30 per nephrology at OSH. On
admission, the patient's Hct was 34.1. Her Hct was trended.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
# Hyponatremia: should follow up with outpatient renal or
primary care if patietn should continue to be fluid restricted
depending on her Na levels.
# Smith:
# Severe aoritc stenosis: patient will follow up with surgery in
outpatient setting after kidney function has resolved back to
baseline
# ACE-I: we held her ACE-I while here because of Smith she will
likely need to restart this as soon as her Cr improves back to
baseline
# Retinal hyperpigmentation: felt to be likely congenital
hyperpigemented retinal epithelium by ophthalmology, but should
have scheduled follow-up with Dr. Miranda in ophthalmology.

Medications on Admission:
Preadmissions medications listed are incomplete and require
futher investigation.  Information was obtained from Patient
Ralph Roach.

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Tucker, Julie

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary diagnoses: acute on chronic heart failure, acute on
chronic renal failure
Secondary diagnosis: severe aortic stenosis


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mrs. Alexander,

It was a pleasure caring for you while you were hospitalized at
the Healthsouth Rehabilitation Hospital Of Southern Arizona. As you know, you were admitted for
heart failure secondary to your narrowed aortic valve. The heart
surgeons had planned to take you to the operating room but you
unfortunately developed acute kidney failure. You we then
transferred to the CCU (cardiac care unit) to help with your
renal function.  While in the CCU, your sodium levels were low
and we closely monitored them. The surgeons decided that they
will want to do the surgery at a later time when your kidney
function has recovered.

We made the following changes to your medications:
START taking amlodipine and hydralazine for your high blood
pressure
We have INCREASED your dose of labetolol to 300mg TID
START taking Sevelamer to lower your phosphorus level
STOP taking allopurinol, colchicine and lisinopril.

It was a pleasure taking care of you Mrs. Alexander

Followup Instructions:

renal follow-up within the next 24-48hr.
